schools.summary: Gill: this was not of servants, who served six years from their own start, but the sabbatical release of debts, at the end of every seven years.

themes: At the end of every seven years thou shalt make a release
a merciful remission of debts
a type of the gospel jubilee (Luke 4:19)

schools.summary: Gill: this gives the rules to be observed in making the release.
Keil & Delitzsch: he shall not exact it of his neighbour or brother, because it is called the LORD's release.

themes: Every creditor that lendeth ought... shall release it... because it is called the LORD'S release
mercy lifted to a sacred duty
we forgive because forgiven (Matt. 18:33)

schools.summary: Gill: of a foreigner the debt might be exacted, on the seventh year or after. Calvin: it was just that despisers of the Law should not enjoy the sabbatical benefit, since God appointed it for his own people.

themes: Of a foreigner thou mayest exact it again: but that which is thine with thy brother thine hand shall release
the special mercy of the covenant people
a peculiar love within the household (Gal. 6:10)

schools.summary: Gill: the law was never meant to screen the able from paying just debts, but only the truly poor; were they obedient, there would be no debts to release, the LORD greatly blessing them.

themes: Save when there shall be no poor among you; for the LORD shall greatly bless thee
the ideal bound to obedience
God's abundance banishing want (Acts 4:34)

schools.summary: Gill: this being the case, there would be no more poor among them, or they would be so blessed as to release the poor's debts without hurting themselves.
Keil & Delitzsch: the condition of careful hearkening unto the voice of the LORD.

themes: Only if thou carefully hearken unto the voice of the LORD thy God, to observe to do all these commandments
the blessing conditioned on obedience
the way of obedience the way of blessing (Deut. 28:1)

schools.summary: Gill: God is faithful who promised, and gives the blessing according to the nature of the promise; conditional temporal promises given as the condition is performed; they would lend to many nations and not borrow.

themes: thou shalt lend unto many nations, but thou shalt not borrow
the dignity of a people that walks with God
made rich to give and bless others (2 Cor. 9:11)

schools.summary: Gill: there would be poor among them if they did not keep the commandments, as the Targums say.
Ellicott: the poor of one's own city come first, 'the poor of thine own city before the poor of another.'

themes: thou shalt not harden thine heart, nor shut thine hand from thy poor brother
charity an inward compassion before an outward act
the shut hand betraying the hard heart (1 John 3:17)

schools.summary: Gill: the hand is to be opened wide and given bountifully, the heart first opened, the affections moved, a willing mind disposed to give generously.
Keil & Delitzsch: the lending of sufficient for his need.

themes: thou shalt open thine hand wide unto him, and shalt surely lend him sufficient for his need
a free, ample liberality from an opened heart
giving full and proportioned to need (2 Cor. 9:7)

schools.summary: Gill: the 'heart of Belial' — the worthless heart without the yoke of God's law, in which Satan works to incline to evil thoughts; the evil thought was to refuse the poor as the year of release drew near.

themes: Beware that there be not a thought in thy wicked heart... The seventh year... is at hand... and thou givest him nought
covetousness dressed as prudence
love gives where no return is hoped (Luke 6:35)

schools.summary: Gill: he must surely give, even a hundred times if asked, to the limit of what is sufficient for present want; the heart not grieved, and God would bless him for it.

themes: Thou shalt surely give him, and thine heart shall not be grieved... the LORD thy God shall bless thee
cheerful, ungrudging giving
the cheerful giver loved and rewarded (2 Cor. 9:7)

schools.summary: Gill: there would always be such objects for charity; had they been obedient there would have been none, but God foresaw they would not keep his commands, so the poor remain.

themes: For the poor shall never cease out of the land... Thou shalt open thine hand wide unto thy brother
the abiding poor a standing call to charity
the poor always with us (Matt. 26:11)

schools.summary: Gill: a man might be sold by the sanhedrim for theft, or a father might sell his daughter under age, or one might sell himself for poverty; after six years' service he was to go free in the seventh.

themes: if thy brother, an Hebrew... be sold unto thee, and serve thee six years; then in the seventh year thou shalt let him go free
a merciful bound to bondage
the brethren a free and redeemed people

schools.summary: Gill: this was when he discharged him from servitude and made him a free man.
Keil & Delitzsch: thou shalt not let him go away empty.

themes: when thou sendest him out free from thee, thou shalt not let him go away empty
liberty not left barren
the freed man furnished, not abandoned

schools.summary: Gill: 'loading thou shalt load him' — give as much as he can carry, to supply his present want and set him up in the world, of that wherewith the LORD had blessed him.

themes: Thou shalt furnish him liberally out of thy flock, and out of thy floor, and out of thy winepress
the parting gift lavish, not token
freely received, freely give (Matt. 10:8)

schools.summary: Gill: they were used hardly in Egypt, their lives made bitter, and so should show the greater compassion to servants.
Ellicott: remember thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt.

themes: thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman... and the LORD thy God redeemed thee
kindness rooted in their own redemption
the redeemed showing redemption's kindness (Eph. 4:32)

schools.summary: Gill: the servant might say he would not go away, nor quit his service.
Keil & Delitzsch: because he loveth thee and thy house, and is well with thee.

themes: if he say unto thee, I will not go away from thee; because he loveth thee and thine house
glad service preferred to bare liberty
a figure of Christ the willing servant (Ps. 40:7-8)

schools.summary: Gill: the awl was thrust through his ear unto the door — not of the master's house but, by the Targum, of the court of judicature, before whom he declared his desire.
Keil & Delitzsch: the boring of the ear, the servant his for ever.

themes: thou shalt take an awl, and thrust it through his ear unto the door, and he shall be thy servant for ever
the token of a chosen, lifelong service
the opened ear, a type of Christ (Ps. 40:6)

schools.summary: Gill: the master should not grudge him his liberty nor what is given at his dismissal. Calvin: how difficult this law was to the niggardly Jews, whom God reproves for their mean and grudging spirit.

themes: It shall not seem hard unto thee, when thou sendest him away free... he hath been worth a double hired servant
meanness rebuked, generosity no loss
releasing without regret what kindness requires

schools.summary: Gill: this was according to the law of the firstborn already given.
Keil & Delitzsch: the firstlings sanctified, with no work or shearing.

themes: All the firstling males... thou shalt sanctify unto the LORD... no work... nor shear
what is devoted to God kept sacred
the firstborn the LORD's, pointing to Christ (Col. 1:15)

schools.summary: Gill: if of male firstlings, only the priests might eat, being devoted to the Lord; if of the people in common, then of female or second firstlings; eaten before the LORD in the chosen place.

themes: Thou shalt eat it before the LORD thy God year by year in the place which the LORD shall choose
the devoted gift a feast of fellowship
religion clothed in thankful gladness

schools.summary: Gill: if there be any blemish, as lame or blind.
Clarke: God will have both a perfect priest and a perfect offering (Lev. 22:20).

themes: if there be any blemish therein... thou shalt not sacrifice it unto the LORD
God requiring a perfect offering
the unblemished Lamb, Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19)

schools.summary: Gill: though it might not be sacrificed nor eaten as a sacred feast at Jerusalem, it might be eaten as common food in their own houses, by clean and unclean alike, as the roebuck and the hart.

themes: Thou shalt eat it within thy gates: the unclean and the clean person shall eat it alike
the imperfect put to lawful common use
the sacred kept unblemished, nothing wasted

schools.summary: Gill: the blood was not to be eaten but poured upon the ground as water, as taught before (Deut. 12:16); this held even for the firstling eaten as common food.

themes: Only thou shalt not eat the blood thereof; thou shalt pour it upon the ground as water
the abiding ban on blood, the life sacred
pointing to the blood of Christ (Heb. 9:22)
